disclaimer: This content is for educational and immediate support purposes only. It is NOT a substitute for professional mental health care. If you are in immediate danger, call your local emergency number.

crisis_hotlines.name: 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline
crisis_hotlines.description: Free, confidential support for people in suicidal crisis or emotional distress.

crisis_hotlines.name: Crisis Text Line
crisis_hotlines.description: Free crisis counseling via text message. Text HELLO to 741741.

crisis_hotlines.name: Veterans Crisis Line
crisis_hotlines.description: Specialized support for veterans, service members, and their families.

crisis_hotlines.name: Trevor Project
crisis_hotlines.description: Crisis intervention and suicide prevention for LGBTQ+ young people.

crisis_hotlines.name: SAMHSA National Helpline
crisis_hotlines.description: Free referrals and information for mental health and substance use disorders.

crisis_hotlines.name: Trans Lifeline
crisis_hotlines.description: Peer support by and for trans people. No non-consensual active rescue.

crisis_hotlines.name: Disaster Distress Helpline
crisis_hotlines.description: Immediate crisis counseling for people experiencing emotional distress from disasters.

crisis_hotlines.name: National Domestic Violence Hotline
crisis_hotlines.description: Support for victims and survivors of domestic violence.

crisis_hotlines.name: RAINN (Sexual Assault)
crisis_hotlines.description: Nation's largest anti-sexual violence organization. Routes to local provider.

crisis_hotlines.name: Childhelp National Child Abuse Hotline
crisis_hotlines.description: Crisis intervention, information, and referrals for child abuse situations.

crisis_hotlines.name: NAMI Helpline
crisis_hotlines.description: Free information, referrals, and support for mental health conditions.

crisis_hotlines.name: iCall (India)
crisis_hotlines.description: Psychosocial helpline by TISS for emotional support and crisis intervention.

crisis_hotlines.name: Vandrevala Foundation (India)
crisis_hotlines.description: Mental health support helpline available across India.

crisis_hotlines.name: Samaritans (UK/Ireland)
crisis_hotlines.description: Emotional support for anyone in distress or at risk of suicide.

crisis_hotlines.name: Beyond Blue (Australia)
crisis_hotlines.description: Support for anxiety, depression, and suicide prevention.

crisis_hotlines.name: Emergency Services
crisis_hotlines.description: For immediate life-threatening emergencies. Other regions: 999 (UK), 000 (AU), 112 (EU), 108 (India).

five_four_three_two_one.title: 5-4-3-2-1 Sensory Grounding
five_four_three_two_one.subtitle: Bring yourself back to the present moment
five_four_three_two_one.evidence: Engages the prefrontal cortex and activates the parasympathetic nervous system by shifting attention from internal distress to external sensory input.

steps.instruction: Look around and name 5 things you can see.
steps.examples: A clock on the wall, light from a window, a book, your hands, a pattern on the floor.

steps.instruction: Notice 4 things you can physically feel.
steps.examples: The texture of your clothing, the chair beneath you, the floor under your feet, a breeze on your skin.

steps.instruction: Listen for 3 things you can hear.
steps.examples: Traffic outside, a clock ticking, your own breathing, birds, an air conditioner.
Coffee, soap on your hands, fresh air, the scent of a nearby plant.
A sip of water, mint from toothpaste, the taste in your mouth right now.

box_breathing.title: Box Breathing
box_breathing.subtitle: Used by Navy SEALs and first responders
box_breathing.evidence: Activates the vagus nerve and parasympathetic nervous system. Reduces cortisol levels and heart rate within 2-3 cycles.

butterfly_hug.title: Butterfly Hug
butterfly_hug.subtitle: Bilateral stimulation for self-soothing
butterfly_hug.evidence: Based on EMDR bilateral stimulation principles. Activates both brain hemispheres to reduce emotional intensity.
steps: Cross your arms over your chest so your fingertips rest on the front of your shoulders.
Alternately tap your shoulders with your hands, like a butterfly flapping its wings.
Tap slowly and gently at a pace that feels comfortable.
As you tap, breathe slowly. Notice what you feel without judgment.
Continue for 1-2 minutes or until you feel calmer.

body_scan.title: Grounding Body Scan
body_scan.subtitle: Reconnect with your physical body
body_scan.evidence: Shifts attention from rumination to somatic awareness. Activates interoceptive pathways in the insula.
steps: Sit or lie in a comfortable position. Close your eyes if that feels safe.
Bring your attention to your feet. Notice any sensation — warmth, pressure, tingling. Just observe.
Slowly move your attention up through your calves, knees, thighs. Notice without judging.
Continue through your hips, belly, chest. Notice your breathing. You don't need to change it.
Bring awareness to your hands, arms, shoulders. Notice any tension. Let it soften if it wants to.
Move attention to your neck, jaw, face, forehead. Unclench your jaw. Soften your brow.
Expand awareness to your whole body at once. You are here. You are present. You are safe in this moment.

temperature_grounding.title: Temperature Reset
temperature_grounding.subtitle: Use cold to activate the dive reflex
temperature_grounding.evidence: Cold water on the face triggers the mammalian dive reflex, immediately activating the parasympathetic nervous system and lowering heart rate. Part of DBT TIPP skills.
steps: Hold ice cubes in your hands, or splash cold water on your face.
If available, press a cold object (drink can, ice pack) against the back of your neck.
Hold a cold drink and focus on the sensation in your hands.
The shock of cold redirects your nervous system from fight-or-flight to calm.

cognitive_grounding.title: Mental Anchoring
cognitive_grounding.subtitle: Give your thinking brain a task
cognitive_grounding.evidence: Engages the prefrontal cortex by requiring focused cognitive effort, interrupting amygdala-driven emotional spirals.
exercises: Name as many animals as you can that start with the letter B
Pick a color and find everything around you that is that color
Recite lyrics to a favorite song
Describe in detail how to make your favorite meal
Name the capitals of as many countries as you can

safety_plan.title: My Safety Plan
safety_plan.subtitle: Based on the Stanley-Brown Safety Planning Intervention
safety_plan.evidence: The Stanley-Brown Safety Planning Intervention is recommended by the VA, SAMHSA, Joint Commission, and 988 Lifeline. Studies show it reduces suicidal behavior by 43% compared to standard care.
safety_plan.instructions: Complete this plan when you are feeling calm. Keep it somewhere easily accessible — your phone, wallet, or refrigerator. Review and update it regularly.

steps.title: My Warning Signs
steps.prompt: What thoughts, feelings, moods, images, or situations tell me a crisis may be developing?
steps.placeholder: e.g., When I start thinking everyone would be better off without me...

steps.title: My Coping Strategies
steps.prompt: Things I can do on my own to take my mind off my problems without contacting another person:
examples: Go for a walk or run
steps.placeholder: e.g., Take a 15-minute walk, do the 5-4-3-2-1 exercise...

steps.title: Social Distractions
steps.prompt: People and places that provide healthy distraction (I don't need to tell them I'm in crisis):
examples: Call a family member to chat about their day
Go to a gym or class
Spend time in a public place
steps.placeholder: e.g., Call my friend Alex to talk about football...

steps.title: People I Trust
steps.prompt: Specific people I can tell I'm struggling. Include their name and phone number:

steps.title: Professional Resources
steps.prompt: Professionals and agencies I can contact during a crisis:
defaults: 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline — call or text 988
Crisis Text Line — text HELLO to 741741

steps.title: Making My Environment Safe
steps.prompt: Steps I can take to reduce access to things I could use to hurt myself:
examples: Ask someone to hold my medications
Have a friend keep my firearms
Delete harmful content from my phone
steps.placeholder: e.g., Give extra medication to my roommate to hold...

deescalation.title: De-escalation Guide
deescalation.subtitle: How to help someone (or yourself) during intense distress

steps.description: Listen actively without interrupting. Pay attention to both words and body language. Allow silence. Reflect what you hear.
Acknowledge their feelings: 'I can see you're really going through something difficult right now. That makes sense.'
Find common ground. You don't have to agree with everything — agree on the goal: 'We both want you to feel better.'
Work together to find next steps. 'What do you think would help right now?' Restore their sense of control.

dos: Speak calmly, slowly, and at low volume
Respect personal space — stay at arm's length
Keep your body language open and non-threatening
Validate their emotions without agreeing with harmful actions: 'I hear how angry you are'
Offer simple choices to restore sense of control: 'Would you like to sit or stand?'
Name the emotion (affect labeling): 'It sounds like you're feeling overwhelmed' — this reduces amygdala activation
Acknowledge their pain before trying to problem-solve
Ask what they need: 'What would help right now?'
Stay present and patient — de-escalation takes time
donts: Don't say 'calm down' or 'relax' — this invalidates their experience
Don't argue about facts during peak distress
Don't cross arms, point fingers, or stand over them
Don't crowd the person or block exits
Don't escalate with threats or consequences
Don't tell them how they 'should' feel
Don't engage bystanders or create an audience
when_to_call_911: The person is actively hurting themselves or others
The person is unconscious or unresponsive
The person has taken an overdose or ingested something harmful
The person is threatening imminent violence

common_responses.title: Common Trauma Responses Are Normal
common_responses.subtitle: These are normal reactions to abnormal situations — they are not signs of weakness or mental illness.

responses: Negative thoughts about self or world

principles.name: Safety
principles.description: Feeling physically and psychologically safe is the foundation of recovery.

principles.name: Trustworthiness & Transparency
principles.description: Building trust through honesty, consistency, and clear communication.

principles.name: Peer Support
principles.description: Shared experiences create trust, safety, and hope for recovery.

principles.name: Collaboration
principles.description: Healing happens in relationships. Sharing power and decision-making.

principles.name: Empowerment
principles.description: Building on strengths. Fostering belief in resilience and ability to heal.

principles.name: Cultural Sensitivity
principles.description: Recognizing cultural context, historical trauma, and gender-responsive needs.

harmful_practices.title: What NOT to Do
harmful_practices.subtitle: Well-intentioned actions that can actually cause harm

items.practice: Forcing someone to talk about what happened
items.why_harmful: Can re-traumatize. Let people share at their own pace and only what they choose.

items.practice: Saying 'Everything will be okay' or 'It could be worse'
items.why_harmful: Minimizes their experience. Acknowledge their pain instead.

items.practice: Asking 'Why didn't you just leave/fight back/say something?'
items.why_harmful: Implies blame. Trauma responses (freeze, fawn) are involuntary survival mechanisms.
Research shows single-session mandatory debriefing can increase PTSD risk (Cochrane review).
Increases distress and suicidality. Shame is already a core trauma response.

items.practice: Over-promising confidentiality when safety is at risk
items.why_harmful: Be transparent about limits. If someone is in danger, others may need to know.
No evidence base. Creates false security. Use evidence-based safety plans instead.

items.practice: Offering alcohol to 'take the edge off'
items.why_harmful: Worsens trauma outcomes, increases risk of substance use disorder.

when_to_seek_help.title: When to Seek Professional Help
signs: Symptoms persist or worsen after 4 weeks
Unable to perform daily activities (work, school, self-care)
Persistent nightmares or flashbacks that don't decrease
Pre-existing mental health conditions that have worsened

evidence_based_treatments.name: STAIR
evidence_based_treatments.full_name: Skills Training in Affective and Interpersonal Regulation

pfa_overview.title: Psychological First Aid (PFA)
pfa_overview.subtitle: What anyone can do — no clinical training required

items: Listen actively and help them feel calm
Connect with loved ones and services

steps.description: Listen with your eyes, ears, and heart. Allow silence.
Shield from further harm. Establish a sense of safety.
Reconnect with family, friends, and community.
Demonstrate calm, healthy behavior. Show recovery is possible.
Teach about normal stress reactions and simple coping techniques.

high.banner: You are not alone. Help is available right now.
high.message: It sounds like you may be going through an incredibly difficult time. Your life matters, and support is available 24/7. Please reach out to a crisis service — a trained counselor is ready to listen without judgment.

moderate.banner: Support is available if you need it.
moderate.message: What you're feeling is real and valid. You don't have to go through this alone. If you're in distress, trained crisis counselors are available anytime.

low.banner: It's okay to ask for help.
low.message: It sounds like you're going through a tough time. That takes courage to express. If these feelings persist or intensify, please consider reaching out to a professional or crisis service.
